items.html:
The Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005

In England, the main fire safety law for workplaces is the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, often shortened to the Fire Safety Order. It applies to almost every care setting — care homes, supported living and the offices behind a home care service — and covers the safety of everyone who could be affected, including the people you support, visitors and staff.

The Order places most legal duties on a ‘responsible person’. In a care home this is usually the employer, owner or registered manager. Where more than one person has some control of a building, they must co-operate so that fire safety is never left to chance.

Fire risk assessments and your duties

The responsible person must arrange a suitable and sufficient fire risk assessment, keep it up to date, and act on what it finds. A good assessment identifies the fire hazards, decides who is at risk, and sets out the measures that reduce the danger and help people escape.

  • Follow the findings: work in line with the fire procedures the assessment leads to.
  • Co-operate: take part in the training and fire drills your employer arranges.
  • Do not add risk: avoid anything that creates a fire hazard or blocks an escape route.
  • Report concerns: tell your employer about faults, missing signs or blocked exits straight away.

items.question: Under the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, whose legal duty is it to make sure a fire risk assessment is carried out and kept up to date?

answers.text: The ‘responsible person’, usually the employer, owner or registered manager.
answers.feedback: Correct. The Order places this duty on the responsible person, who must arrange the assessment and act on its findings.

answers.text: Each individual care worker, for the rooms they work in.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. You must co-operate and report concerns, but arranging the assessment is the responsible person’s duty, not yours.

answers.text: The local fire and rescue service.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The fire service enforces the law and may inspect, but the duty to assess and manage the risk sits with the responsible person.

answers.text: The people who use the service.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The people you support are those the assessment protects; the legal duty rests with the responsible person.

items.html:
Everyday sources of fire

Most fires in care are prevented long before they start, through small habits that remove heat or fuel. Electrical faults and misuse are a common cause, so treat sockets, leads and appliances with care.

  • Electrical safety: report frayed cables, cracked plugs and scorch marks, and avoid overloading sockets or daisy-chaining extension leads.
  • Charging: charge phones, mobility scooters and battery equipment on a hard surface, never on a bed or sofa, and unplug once charged.
  • Smoking: support people to smoke only in agreed areas, use proper ashtrays, and check that materials are fully out.

Oxygen, cooking, candles and clutter

Some hazards are specific to the people you support and need extra thought.

  • Oxygen: keep cigarettes, cookers and naked flames well away from anyone using oxygen, as it makes fire take hold and spread fiercely.
  • Cooking: never leave a hob or grill unattended, keep tea towels away from heat, and stay alert if someone tends to forget pans on the stove.
  • Candles and heaters: discourage open flames, and keep portable heaters clear of curtains, bedding and clothing.
  • Housekeeping: store waste, laundry and clutter tidily, as a build-up gives a fire more fuel and blocks escape routes.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the fire prevention advice.
items.textField: You should charge a mobility scooter on a *hard* surface and never overload a *socket*. Keep naked flames well away from anyone using *oxygen*, because it makes fire spread fiercely. Portable *heaters* must be kept clear of bedding and curtains, and a build-up of *clutter* should be cleared away because it gives fire extra fuel.

items.html:
Fire classes and the right extinguisher

Fires are grouped into classes by what is burning, and using the wrong extinguisher can make matters far worse. You are not expected to be an expert, but a basic awareness helps you understand the equipment around you and why it is placed where it is.

  • Class A — solids such as paper, wood and fabric: water or foam.
  • Class B — flammable liquids such as petrol or spirits: foam, carbon dioxide or powder.
  • Class C — flammable gases: the supply should be shut off; powder may be used.
  • Electrical fires: carbon dioxide (CO2) suits live electrical equipment and leaves no residue.

Cooking fires and when not to fight

Two further types matter in care settings. Wet chemical extinguishers are made for Class F fires — cooking oils and deep fat — while dry powder is versatile but creates a choking cloud that is unpleasant and hard to see through indoors. Colour-coded labels help you tell them apart.

Fighting a fire is always a last resort. Only ever tackle a small fire if you have been trained, it is not spreading, you have raised the alarm first, and you have a clear escape route at your back. Never use water on electrical equipment or on a cooking-oil fire. If you are in any doubt at all, get out and leave it to the fire service.

items.question: A small fire has started in live electrical equipment. Which extinguisher is most suitable, and what is the safest overall approach?

answers.text: Carbon dioxide (CO2), but only tackle it if trained, it is not spreading and you have raised the alarm and kept an escape route.
answers.feedback: Correct. CO2 suits electrical fires, and fighting any fire is a last resort that depends on training, a contained fire and a clear exit.

answers.text: A water extinguisher, aimed straight at the equipment.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Water conducts electricity and must never be used on live electrical equipment; CO2 is the safer choice.
Incorrect. Wet chemical is designed for cooking-oil (Class F) fires, not electrical equipment.

answers.text: Any extinguisher will do, so grab the nearest one and start spraying.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Using the wrong extinguisher can make a fire worse, and fighting a fire is only ever a last resort when it is safe to do so.

items.html:
Progressive horizontal evacuation and fire doors

Many care buildings are designed so that people do not have to leave the building straight away. Progressive horizontal evacuation means moving people sideways, through fire doors into an adjoining fire compartment on the same floor, away from the fire but still in a place of relative safety. This suits people who are frail or hard to move quickly, and it buys time.

Fire doors are central to this. They hold back smoke and flames for a set period, giving people time to move to safety. Never wedge or prop a fire door open, and make sure nothing stops it closing fully.

Stay put, simultaneous evacuation and PEEPs

Your fire risk assessment sets the evacuation strategy for the building, and you must know which one applies where you work.

  • Simultaneous evacuation: everyone leaves at once to the assembly point when the alarm sounds — common in smaller settings.
  • Stay put: in some buildings, people in unaffected areas stay where they are while the fire is contained, unless staff or the fire service tell them to move.
  • Assembly point: gather at the agreed place so everyone can be accounted for, and never go back inside for any reason.

A Personal Emergency Evacuation Plan (PEEP) records the help each person needs — equipment such as an evacuation sledge, how many staff, and their communication needs — so no one is overlooked in an emergency.

items.question: It is acceptable to wedge a fire door open on a warm day, as long as staff are working nearby.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Fire doors must be free to close so they can hold back smoke and flames; wedging one open removes that protection and puts everyone at risk.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. This is false: fire doors must always be able to close fully, because a propped-open door lets smoke and fire spread and undermines the whole evacuation strategy.

items.html:
The fire warden role and drills

Many services appoint fire wardens, sometimes called fire marshals, to take a lead if the alarm sounds. It is a support and co-ordination role, not a firefighting one. A warden typically helps direct people to the exits, checks that rooms and toilets are clear where it is safe to do so, and reports to the person in charge at the assembly point.

Fire drills give everyone the chance to practise before a real emergency. Take them seriously, learn your nearest exits and the sound of the alarm, and treat every alarm as real until you are told otherwise.

After a fire

Once everyone is out and the fire service has taken over, there is still important work to do.

  • Account for people: confirm who has been evacuated and tell the fire service immediately about anyone unaccounted for.
  • Do not go back: only the fire service decides when a building is safe to re-enter.
  • Support people: keep those you care for warm, calm and reassured, as a fire is frightening and disorientating.
  • Record and review: report what happened so the incident can be recorded and the fire risk assessment reviewed and improved.

items.question: It is a night shift in a care home that uses progressive horizontal evacuation. The alarm sounds and you find smoke coming from a resident’s bedroom on your corridor. What is the best action?

answers.text: Raise the alarm, close the door on the fire, and help move people through the fire doors into the next compartment while 999 is called.
answers.feedback: Correct. Raising the alarm, containing the fire by closing the door and moving people to an adjoining compartment fits a progressive horizontal evacuation and puts people first.

answers.text: Go into the bedroom and try to put the fire out before waking anyone.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Warning people and getting them to safety comes first; a fire producing smoke in a bedroom is not a small contained fire to tackle alone.

answers.text: Open the windows in the corridor to clear the smoke.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Extra air feeds a fire, and the priority is to raise the alarm and move people to safety, not to ventilate the area.

answers.text: Help residents gather their belongings before leaving the corridor.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. There is no time to collect belongings; people must be moved to safety first, as possessions can be replaced but lives cannot.
